title: Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
subtitle: Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a [terrorist attack](#dod:terrorism).
caption:
Share who are worried about vs. share of deaths from terrorism
Respondents who said they worried "very much" or "a great deal". Confirmed deaths, including all victims and attackers who died because of a terrorist attack.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between the share of people worried about terrorism and the share of deaths from terrorism across countries, with circle sizes indicating the number of attacks. The horizontal axis plots the share of deaths from terrorism on a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.0001% to 1%, while the vertical axis measures the share of people worried about terrorism from 30% to 90%. Countries are color-coded by region: North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. Iraq and Nigeria show the highest shares of deaths from terrorism, both positioned near the 1% mark on the horizontal axis, with Iraq's worry level around 85% and Nigeria's near 90%. Myanmar displays the highest worry level at approximately 95% despite a relatively low death share around 0.01%. China and Germany cluster at the lower end of the death share scale, both below 0.001%, with worry levels of 65% and 60% respectively. New Zealand shows the lowest worry level at roughly 35% with a death share near 0.1%. The United Kingdom, Netherlands, and Canada all fall below 50% worry levels with death shares under 0.001%. European countries generally occupy the lower-left quadrant with low death shares and moderate worry levels, while several Asian and African nations show higher worry levels across varying death share ranges. The distribution shows no clear linear relationship between the share of deaths from terrorism and the share of people worried about terrorism across the countries plotted.
